primer.markdown:
# The three states of an agent loop: end_turn, tool_use, iterate

Module 02 taught you to declare a tool schema. Module 04 teaches you what the agent actually *does* when Claude decides to call it. The answer is a tight `while (true)` loop with exactly two exit/continuation branches, keyed on `response.stop_reason`.

The typescript/GUIDE.md loop on lines 388-440 is the canonical shape. You initialize `messages` with the user's turn. Inside the loop you call `messages.create` with the tools array. The response comes back with a `stop_reason`. If it is `"end_turn"`, Claude has produced the final text — return it and exit. If it is `"tool_use"`, the response's `content` array contains one or more `tool_use` blocks, each with a `name`, an `id`, and an `input` object that validates against your schema. You **append the assistant's message verbatim** (`messages.push({ role: "assistant", content: response.content })`), then **execute the tool function** (a lookup by `block.name`), then **append a `user` message whose content is an array of `tool_result` blocks** (one per tool call, correlated by `tool_use_id` to the original `id`), then loop. Claude sees the tool results on the next iteration and either calls another tool or returns `end_turn`.

The two loop invariants that matter: (1) every `tool_use` must be answered by a `tool_result` with the same `tool_use_id` — the SDK rejects the next request otherwise; (2) the assistant turn you push back must be `response.content` unchanged, not a re-serialisation. Break either invariant and the loop either 400s immediately or silently drops context.
primer.key_insight: The agent loop is a state machine with two labels: `end_turn` exits, `tool_use` executes-and-continues. The tool_result block ties the loop back to the tool_use block via `tool_use_id` — that ID is the single thread that keeps the conversation coherent.

visual.alt: A state machine diagram. A rounded box labelled `messages.create(tools)` emits an arrow to a diamond labelled `stop_reason?`. The diamond has two outgoing edges. The left edge is labelled `end_turn` and ends at a terminal node marked `return response.content[0].text`. The right edge is labelled `tool_use` and leads through three sequential boxes — `push assistant message`, `execute block.input`, `push user turn with tool_result[tool_use_id=block.id]` — before looping back with a curved arrow to the original `messages.create(tools)` box.
visual.alt_es: Un diagrama de maquina de estados. Una caja redondeada etiquetada `messages.create(tools)` emite una flecha a un rombo etiquetado `stop_reason?`. El rombo tiene dos aristas de salida. La arista izquierda etiquetada `end_turn` termina en un nodo terminal marcado `return response.content[0].text`. La arista derecha etiquetada `tool_use` pasa por tres cajas secuenciales — `push assistant message`, `execute block.input`, `push user turn with tool_result[tool_use_id=block.id]` — antes de regresar con una flecha curva a la caja original `messages.create(tools)`.

sandbox_policy.csp: default-src 'none'; style-src 'unsafe-inline' https://fonts.googleapis.com; script-src 'unsafe-inline'; img-src data:; font-src https://fonts.gstatic.com; connect-src 'none'; frame-ancestors 'self'

interactive.success_criterion: Learner reads the annotated loop, identifies the two exit/continuation branches on `stop_reason`, and can explain why `tool_use_id` must correlate to `block.id` in the tool_result.

questions.prompt: In typescript/GUIDE.md lines 388-440, what value of `response.stop_reason` causes `runWithTools` to return its final answer and exit the loop?
questions.explanation: GUIDE.md lines 404-407: `if (response.stop_reason === "end_turn")` returns the text block. `"tool_use"` (line 410) is the *continue* branch — it means Claude wants to call a tool and expects you to loop. `"max_tokens"` would mean the cap you set on line 396 was hit; the example does not handle that case.

questions.prompt: On GUIDE.md line 430, each `tool_result` block carries `tool_use_id: block.id`. What happens if you drop that field or use a different value?
options: Nothing — the API matches tool calls by order.
The next `messages.create` call rejects the request because it cannot correlate the tool_result to a prior tool_use.
Claude silently ignores the result and the loop runs forever.
questions.explanation: GUIDE.md line 430: `tool_use_id: block.id` is the correlation key. The API pairs every `tool_use` block (which has an `id`) with exactly one `tool_result` block (whose `tool_use_id` matches). A missing or mismatched ID yields a 400 from the API on the next iteration.

questions.prompt: Why does GUIDE.md line 412 push `response.content` verbatim into `messages` as the assistant turn, instead of just pushing the text?
options: Because the SDK mutates `response.content` and a shallow reference is required.
Because pushing text alone is shorter and equivalent.
Because `response.content` is an array of content blocks (including the original `tool_use` block) that Claude needs to see in its own history to match its next `tool_result` against.
questions.explanation: GUIDE.md line 412: `messages.push({ role: "assistant", content: response.content })`. The assistant's content includes the `tool_use` blocks verbatim. On the next turn, Claude reads its own previous `tool_use` and the user's paired `tool_result` to know what happened. Replacing `response.content` with just the text strips the `tool_use` block and breaks the pairing.
